info: The Mesolithic climax: oak and elm dominated a mostly forested landscape, with alder carr defining the shrinking margins of Doggerland.
background: The most structurally forested period yet — oak and elm dominated the canopy across much of the map, with lime advancing through the warmest and most oceanic western areas and alder colonising river valleys and wet ground rapidly. Alder carr became a defining feature of floodplains and coastal lowlands, including the margins of the shrinking North Sea land bridge. This is the Mesolithic climax — the period when the hunter-gatherer populations of northwest Europe reached their greatest density and ecological sophistication, living in a landscape that was predominantly forested but structured by their own burning and hunting practices. Structurally this forest was denser than the Eemian natural baseline — a consequence of megafauna loss rather than a natural climax state. Perhaps 15–25% fully open ground remained, concentrated along coasts, wetlands and actively managed zones. Doggerland was by now significantly reduced by rising sea levels, its final remnants likely a mosaic of reed marsh, alder carr and open water.
